diagrams.title: Servlet Lifecycle Methods Flow
diagrams.description: A horizontal flowchart showing the lifecycle of a servlet from initialization to destruction
elements: Servlet Container loads the servlet class
Constructor is called - creates servlet instance
init() method is invoked - initializes servlet config
For each client request: service() method is called, which dispatches to doGet()/doPost()
Container decides to remove servlet: destroy() method is called - cleanup resources
Servlet class is unloaded from memory
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete lifecycle of a servlet and when each method is invoked. Essential for exam questions on servlet lifecycle and thread safety considerations.

diagrams.title: HTTP Request-Response Flow with Servlet Methods
diagrams.description: A vertical diagram showing how HTTP requests flow through servlet methods to generate responses
elements: Client Browser sends HTTP Request (GET/POST)
Tomcat Server receives request
service(request, response) method is called
service() checks request type and calls appropriate handler: doGet() or doPost()
Servlet processes request using: getParameter(), getRequestDispatcher(), etc.
Servlet creates response using: setContentType(), getWriter(), etc.
Response sent back to client via Tomcat Server
HTTP Response displayed in browser
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete flow from client request to server response, including which methods are involved at each stage. Helps in debugging and understanding request handling.

diagrams.title: Servlet API Class Hierarchy
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the inheritance structure of servlet classes and interfaces
elements: java.lang.Object (root)
GenericServlet (abstract class) implements Servlet, ServletConfig, Serializable
HttpServlet (abstract class) extends GenericServlet
User-defined Servlet class extends HttpServlet
Servlet Interface: init(), service(), destroy(), getServletConfig(), getServletInfo()
ServletConfig Interface: getInitParameter(), getServletContext(), getServletName()
HttpServletRequest Interface: getParameter(), getMethod(), getSession()
HttpServletResponse Interface: setContentType(), getWriter(), addCookie()
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the inheritance hierarchy and know which methods are inherited at each level. Important for understanding method overriding and interface implementation.

diagrams.title: JSP Lifecycle Methods Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing JSP page translation and lifecycle phases
diagrams.description2: JSP page is requested by client
elements: JSP page is requested by client
Tomcat checks if JSP file has been translated to servlet
If not translated: JSP is translated to _jsp.java servlet source file
_jsp.java is compiled to _jsp.class bytecode
Servlet instance is created via jspInit() method
For each request: _jspService(request, response) is called - handles HTTP method
When container shuts down: jspDestroy() is called - cleanup
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how JSP files are converted to servlets internally and when jspInit, _jspService, and jspDestroy methods are invoked.

diagrams.title: Reading Servlet Parameters Flow
diagrams.description: A diagram showing how HTML form data is read using getParameter() method
elements: HTML Form with input fields (username, password)
Form submits via GET or POST method
Tomcat creates HttpServletRequest object
Servlet extracts parameter: String user = request.getParameter("username")
If multiple values: String[] hobbies = request.getParameterValues("hobby")
Parameter names and values stored in request object as key-value pairs
Servlet processes data and generates response
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to retrieve user input from HTML forms in servlets. Essential for building form-handling web applications.

charts.title: doGet() vs doPost() Method Comparison
charts.content:
Purpose: Compare HTTP GET and POST methods in servlets

| Aspect | doGet() | doPost() |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Method Type | GET request | POST request |
| Data Location | Query string (URL) | Request body |
| Data Size | Limited (~240 chars) | Unlimited (no limit) |
| Security | Less secure (visible in URL) | More secure (hidden) |
| Bookmarkable | Yes | No |
| Caching | Cached by browsers | Not cached |
| Idempotent | Yes | No |
| Default Behavior | Yes (if not overridden) | Must explicitly override |
| Use Case | Search, navigation | Form submission, file upload |
| Speed | Faster | Slightly slower |

Exam Tip: Questions frequently ask when to use doGet vs doPost and the differences between them.

charts.title: Servlet Lifecycle Decision Flow
charts.content:
Purpose: Decision flowchart for servlet lifecycle management

[Start] → [Container Starts] → [Load Servlet Class] → [Create Instance (constructor)] → [Call init()] → [Ready for Requests]

[For Each Request] → [service() called] → [Is GET request?] → Yes → [doGet()] → [Generate Response]
 ↓ No
 [Is POST request?] → Yes → [doPost()] → [Generate Response]
 ↓ No
 [Other methods: doPut, doDelete, doHead, doOptions, doTrace]

[Container Shuts Down] → [destroy() called] → [Release resources] → [Unload Servlet Class] → [End]

Note: service() method is thread-safe if implemented properly; doGet/doPost are not thread-safe by default.

charts.title: Servlet API Methods Classification
charts.content:
Purpose: Categorize all important servlet methods by functionality

Lifecycle Methods:
• init() - Called once during initialization
• service() - Called for each request (dispatches to doXxx)
• destroy() - Called during shutdown

Request Handling Methods:
• doGet() - Handle GET requests
• doPost() - Handle POST requests
• doPut() - Handle PUT requests
• doDelete() - Handle DELETE requests
• doHead() - Handle HEAD requests
• doOptions() - Handle OPTIONS requests
• doTrace() - Handle TRACE requests

Request Information Methods:
• getParameter() - Single parameter value
• getParameterValues() - Multiple values
• getParameterNames() - All parameter names
• getMethod() - GET/POST/PUT/etc
• getRequestURI() - Requested URL

Response Methods:
• setContentType() - Set MIME type
• getWriter() - Get PrintWriter
• setStatus() - Set HTTP status code
• sendRedirect() - Redirect to another URL
• setHeader() - Set response header

Dispatcher Methods:
• getRequestDispatcher() - Forward request
• include() - Include another resource

charts.title: Servlet vs JSP Lifecycle Methods
charts.content:
Purpose: Compare servlet and JSP lifecycle methods

| Aspect | Servlet | JSP |
|--------|---------|-----|
| File Extension | .java | .jsp |
| Translation | Not required | Translated to servlet |
| init() equivalent | init() | jspInit() |
| service() equivalent | service() | _jspService() |
| destroy() equivalent | destroy() | jspDestroy() |
| Default Method | service() handles all | _jspService() handles all |
| Override | Override any method | Override jspInit/jspDestroy only |
| Thread Safety | Manual handling needed | Less control |
| When Called | Per request | Translation + per request |
| Developer Override | Full control | jspInit/jspDestroy only |

Key Point: JSP is translated to servlet internally; jspInit() and jspDestroy() are the only lifecycle methods developers should override.

branches: HTTP Request Handler Methods (doGet, doPost, doPut, doDelete)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview of all methods in servlet and JSP technology. Students can use this for quick revision and to understand relationships between different method categories. Essential for exam preparation as it covers the entire method landscape of Module 4.
